sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:

Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: UP didn't move anything. Both squares stayed put. UP only works at certain columns (col 21 worked before). Right square at col 36 is stuck vertically.
- Recent findings: Blue squares moved from row 51 to row 46 (up 5 rows again). They're moving up consistently through the black shape's "legs".
- Plan: Try RIGHT to move right square further left, then test UP again from new position.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 48 actions; a strong score needs about 30 or fewer. You are 1.6x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
RIGHT didn't move anything either! Both squares stayed at (41, 21) and (46, 36). So RIGHT only works when the square is at row 46 (the right leg's opening). The right square at (46, 36) didn't move with RIGHT.
Let me reconsider the mechanics:
- UP worked when left square was at (46, 21) → moved to (41, 21)
- RIGHT worked when right square was at (46, 41) → moved to (46, 36)
- Now RIGHT doesn't work at (46, 36)
- UP doesn't work at (46, 36) or (41, 21)
It seems like each square can only move in certain directions depending on its position. The movement seems to be constrained by the black shape's structure.
Let me think about what columns/rows allow movement:
- Left square: UP works at col 21 (from row 46 to 41)
- Right square: RIGHT works at row 46 (from col 41 to 36)
Maybe the squares need to be at the "opening" of the legs to move. The left leg opening is at row 46, col 21. The right leg opening is at row 46, cols 36-41.
Since RIGHT didn't work at (46, 36), maybe the right square needs to be at a different position. Or maybe I need to move the left square back down first.
Let me try LEFT to move the left square from (41, 21). If LEFT works, it might move the left square horizontally.
Actually, let me reconsider. Maybe the goal is to get both squares to the same position or to specific target locations. Let me look at the board structure more carefully.
The black figure has two "legs" with openings at the bottom. The blue squares are in these openings. The left leg has a yellow region above it, and the right leg has an orange region.
Maybe I need to move both squares UP into the colored regions. The left square is already at (41, 21) which is in the yellow region. The right square at (46, 36) needs to go up into the orange region.
Let me try DOWN on the left square to bring it back to row 46, then try to move both squares together somehow.
Actually wait - let me re-examine. When both squares were at row 46 (initially at cols 21 and 41), UP moved both up. Then RIGHT moved the right one. Then UP only moved the left one. 
So maybe:
- When squares are at the same row, directional moves affect both
- When squares are at different rows, directional moves only affect one
Let me try DOWN to bring the left square back to row 46, so both are at the same row again, then try UP to move both up together.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: RIGHT and UP both had no effect. Squares at (41, 21) and (46, 36). Movement seems position-dependent - each square only responds to certain directions at certain locations.
Plan: Try DOWN to bring left square back to row 46 so both squares align, then UP might move both.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Try DOWN to bring left square back down
result = action(['DOWN'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
blues = []
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'S':
        center_r = sum(p[0] for p in n['boundary'])/len(n['boundary'])
        center_c = sum(p[1] for p in n['boundary'])/len(n['boundary'])
        blues.append((center_r, center_c))
blues.sort()
print(f"After DOWN: blues at {blues}")

# If both at same row, try UP
if len(blues) == 2 and abs(blues[0][0] - blues[1][0]) < 1:
    result2 = action(['UP'])
    seg2 = current_frame.segmentation
    blues2 = []
    for n in seg2['nodes']:
        if n['color'] == 'S':
            center_r = sum(p[0] for p in n['boundary'])/len(n['boundary'])
            center_c = sum(p[1] for p in n['boundary'])/len(n['boundary'])
            blues2.append((center_r, center_c))
    blues2.sort()
    print(f"After UP: blues at {blues2}")
    
print(f"Done: {last_action_result.get('done')}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python
sections.content:
After DOWN: blues at [(46.0, 21.0), (51.0, 36.0)]
Done: False
